title: My husband’s suicide shows there’s something very wrong with the U.S. insurance industry
excerpt: “My love for Randy and my professional experience were not enough to save him,” a physician writes about her husband’s suicide.
content_html:
I remember standing outside an exam room, phone vibrating in my breast pocket, when the coroner’s call came through. I hadn’t wanted to believe the neighbor’s frantic text about a body bag removed from my husband’s condo, but the medical examiner’s office on my caller ID was unmistakable. I answered, and sounds fell out of my mouth into the phone: “Hello, this is Dr. Hardison.”

I am an emergency physician living in the U.S., and we had one of the top commercial health insurance plans. When my husband, Randy, became suicidal for the first time in his life, I thought I could find him the best care, our insurance would pay for it, and he would get better. Only one of those three things actually happened.

The coroner confirmed it was suicide by asphyxiation. He asked if I could send a recent photo to help ID the body. “In a few minutes I can — I have a patient waiting.” I put the intrusive visual of Randy’s bloated face into a compartment of my mind that was sealed off from the task at hand.

On the other side of the exam room door was a toddler propped in her mother’s arms, coughing between guppy breaths. My face rearranged into calm, and I greeted the mother and child as my eyes darted to the monitor showing low oxygen and fast pulse — signs of respiratory distress. I put the child on oxygen and ordered a breathing treatment and a chest X-ray. The little girl gripped my index finger as I listened to her lungs, and we looked at each other with wonder. Each rise and fall of her chest seemed a miracle.

I felt safer in that exam room than in the outside world. I knew what to do in that room, and in that room, I could make things better. Outside of that room, all bets were off.

“I’d like to test her for influenza and RSV,” I said to the mother.

“Will our insurance cover that?” She mouthed it to me so her daughter wouldn’t hear.

“I hope so,” I told her. “I don’t know why they wouldn’t.”

As I stepped out of the exam room, I became aware of my veins pulsing and a white noise filling my head. Chest paralyzed, I didn’t scream. Instead, I paced down the hall and out the metal door to sit on the curb, waiting for breath to come.

I had fallen in love with Randy because his optimism was magnetic. When his startup began achieving significant milestones after years of sweat equity, he had even more reason to be optimistic. I still have the photo of Randy and his co-founder posing with their first large check, their faces painted with true bliss.

Months later the success fizzled, but Randy never told me how bad things were until the demise of the business was inevitable. The way he tried to protect me from the truth created an ever-expanding distance between us. Each day he withdrew deeper inside an impenetrable shell, and my attempts to connect were met with anger.

As a doctor I should have seen this behavior as a sign of burgeoning depression, but instead I felt wounded by his abrasive tone. I grew fearful of asking questions because they were met with irritability.

One Friday morning, less than four months before the coroner’s phone call, Randy texted me from our home office: I need help. When I opened the glass French door, I was shocked to find Randy on the daybed in a fetal position.

“You have to hide me,” he stuttered. “The investors … they’re coming for me. You have to get me out of here.”

“Randy, that makes no sense. Just talk to them. What’s happened?”

“I can’t do this anymore. Please kill me. Just put this pillow over my face and suffocate me, please. If you don’t do it, I will!” he threatened, grasping and shaking the pillow with both hands. His knuckles were blanching from the pressure of his grip.

Randy needed immediate psychiatric care, and as an emergency physician I knew the options. We put our trust in the mental health team at a local crisis receiving unit, and they kept Randy overnight in temporary safe housing where he could be observed. By morning they had devised a plan to transfer Randy to a retreat-like inpatient psychiatric facility that was in-network with our insurance. The psychiatrist felt Randy was very high risk, but that he could heal with eight weeks of inpatient treatment. We were told our insurance would cover it.

I remember giving Randy a long hug goodbye outside the brick intake building where family members were not allowed. I remember how we lingered, and how he clung to me. I didn’t know it would be our last embrace. He told me he felt good about this place. It seemed peaceful. He would work hard to heal, for me and for the kids.

I felt good about the program, too. I had researched it heavily and agreed it was the best option.

But six days into Randy’s eight-week hospital stay, our health insurance company denied any further inpatient care. Randy had just started to feel hopeful, and he was devastated by the news. I was incredulous. How could they go against the psychiatrist’s recommendations? Couldn’t they see how risky that was?

The facility appealed Randy’s case, providing psychiatrist’s notes that clearly outlined his need, but the outcome did not change. Because Randy had no prior history of mental illness, the insurance company determined he shouldn’t need to be hospitalized and stopped paying for his care. A day later Randy was forcibly discharged.

The ensuing months led to a rotating cycle of failed outpatient treatment programs followed by ER visits, brief admissions, and premature discharges. And Randy kept getting worse. He became so unstable with paranoid delusions and psychosis that he was not safe to be around me or the children. He never came home.

A few days before Randy was found dead, he was supposed to have a video chat with our son. Sitting on my lap in his dinosaur pajamas, little green legs swinging back and forth as I wrapped my arms around him, our son turned to look up at me and asked, “Is Daddy too tired to talk?” I gave him a squeeze and rubbed his back. As the minutes ticked by, Randy didn’t appear on the screen. My son became heavier in my lap, his eyes drifting closed.

When I listen to lungs with my stethoscope, I still feel wonder at the rise and fall of each person’s breath, and my responsibility to secure safe passage to the other side of every crisis weighs heavily. But I can only control what happens inside the exam room.

My love for Randy and my professional experience were not enough to save him. I am a physician, and yet my husband died, proving he was sick enough to deserve care.

Now every time a patient asks me, “will this be covered by my insurance?” the feeling of panic returns.

With Randy in my heart, I keep advocating for what is right. During the day on insurance appeal calls. Late at night, fighting sleep to finish charts and check the right boxes so claims won’t get denied.

Joy Evers (née Hardison), M.D., M.P.H., is a board-certified emergency physician, Mayo Medical School graduate, host of the podcast “Your Doctor Wants to Quit,” and founder of Healthtopia Clinics, an integrative primary care practice serving over 35,000 people in Southern California.

If you or someone you know may be considering suicide, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline: Call or text 988 or chat 988lifeline.org. For TTY users: Use your preferred relay service or dial 711 then 988.

blocks.html_en: I remember standing outside an exam room, phone vibrating in my breast pocket, when the coroner’s call came through. I hadn’t wanted to believe the neighbor’s frantic text about a body bag removed from my husband’s condo, but the medical examiner’s office on my caller ID was unmistakable. I answered, and sounds fell out of my mouth into the phone: “Hello, this is Dr. Hardison.”

sentences.en: I remember standing outside an exam room, phone vibrating in my breast pocket, when the coroner’s call came through.
sentences.zh: 我记得当验尸官的电话打来时，我站在检查室外，胸前口袋里的手机震动着。

sentences.en: I hadn’t wanted to believe the neighbor’s frantic text about a body bag removed from my husband’s condo, but the medical examiner’s office on my caller ID was unmistakable.
sentences.zh: 我不想相信邻居发来的关于从我丈夫的公寓里搬走尸袋的疯狂短信，但我的来电显示上的法医办公室是明确无误的。

sentences.en: I answered, and sounds fell out of my mouth into the phone: “Hello, this is Dr. Hardison.”
sentences.zh: 我接了电话，声音从我嘴里传到电话里：“你好，我是哈迪森医生。”

blocks.html_en: I am an emergency physician living in the U.S., and we had one of the top commercial health insurance plans. When my husband, Randy, became suicidal for the first time in his life, I thought I could find him the best care, our insurance would pay for it, and he would get better. Only one of those three things actually happened.

sentences.en: I am an emergency physician living in the U.S., and we had one of the top commercial health insurance plans.
sentences.zh: 我是一名住在美国的急诊医生，我们拥有顶级的商业健康保险计划之一。

sentences.en: When my husband, Randy, became suicidal for the first time in his life, I thought I could find him the best care, our insurance would pay for it, and he would get better.
sentences.zh: 当我的丈夫兰迪一生中第一次出现自杀倾向时，我以为我可以为他找到最好的护理，我们的保险会支付费用，他会好起来的。

sentences.en: Only one of those three things actually happened.
sentences.zh: 这三件事中只发生了一件。

blocks.html_en: The coroner confirmed it was suicide by asphyxiation. He asked if I could send a recent photo to help ID the body. “In a few minutes I can — I have a patient waiting.” I put the intrusive visual of Randy’s bloated face into a compartment of my mind that was sealed off from the task at hand.

sentences.en: The coroner confirmed it was suicide by asphyxiation.
sentences.zh: 验尸官确认是窒息自杀。

sentences.en: He asked if I could send a recent photo to help ID the body.
sentences.zh: 他问我是否可以发送一张最近的照片来帮助识别尸体。

sentences.en: “In a few minutes I can — I have a patient waiting.”
sentences.zh: “几分钟后我就可以——我有一个病人在等我。”

sentences.en: I put the intrusive visual of Randy’s bloated face into a compartment of my mind that was sealed off from the task at hand.
sentences.zh: 我把兰迪肿胀的脸的侵入性视觉放入我脑海中的一个隔间中，与手头的任务隔离开来。

blocks.html_en: On the other side of the exam room door was a toddler propped in her mother’s arms, coughing between guppy breaths. My face rearranged into calm, and I greeted the mother and child as my eyes darted to the monitor showing low oxygen and fast pulse — signs of respiratory distress. I put the child on oxygen and ordered a breathing treatment and a chest X-ray. The little girl gripped my index finger as I listened to her lungs, and we looked at each other with wonder. Each rise and fall of her chest seemed a miracle.

sentences.en: On the other side of the exam room door was a toddler propped in her mother’s arms, coughing between guppy breaths.
sentences.zh: 检查室门的另一边是一个蹒跚学步的孩子，靠在母亲的怀里，用孔雀鱼般的呼吸声咳嗽着。

sentences.en: My face rearranged into calm, and I greeted the mother and child as my eyes darted to the monitor showing low oxygen and fast pulse — signs of respiratory distress.
sentences.zh: 我的表情重新变得平静，我向母亲和孩子打招呼，同时我的眼睛飞快地盯着显示器，显示低氧和快速脉搏——呼吸窘迫的迹象。

sentences.en: I put the child on oxygen and ordered a breathing treatment and a chest X-ray.
sentences.zh: 我给孩子吸氧，并要求进行呼吸治疗和胸部 X 光检查。

sentences.en: The little girl gripped my index finger as I listened to her lungs, and we looked at each other with wonder.
sentences.zh: 当我听她的肺部声音时，小女孩握住我的食指，我们惊讶地看着对方。

sentences.en: Each rise and fall of her chest seemed a miracle.
sentences.zh: 她胸口的每一次起伏都像是一个奇迹。

blocks.html_en: I felt safer in that exam room than in the outside world. I knew what to do in that room, and in that room, I could make things better. Outside of that room, all bets were off.

sentences.en: I felt safer in that exam room than in the outside world.
sentences.zh: 我在那个考场里感觉比在外面的世界更安全。

sentences.en: I knew what to do in that room, and in that room, I could make things better.
sentences.zh: 我知道在那个房间里要做什么，在那个房间里，我可以让事情变得更好。

sentences.en: Outside of that room, all bets were off.
sentences.zh: 在那个房间之外，所有的赌注都被取消了。

blocks.html_en: “I’d like to test her for influenza and RSV,” I said to the mother.

sentences.en: “I’d like to test her for influenza and RSV,” I said to the mother.
sentences.zh: “我想对她进行流感和呼吸道合胞病毒检测，”我对母亲说。

blocks.html_en: “Will our insurance cover that?” She mouthed it to me so her daughter wouldn’t hear.

sentences.en: “Will our insurance cover that?”
sentences.zh: “我们的保险可以承保吗？”

sentences.en: She mouthed it to me so her daughter wouldn’t hear.
sentences.zh: 她用嘴对我说，以免她的女儿听到。

blocks.html_en: “I hope so,” I told her. “I don’t know why they wouldn’t.”

sentences.en: “I hope so,” I told her.
sentences.zh: “我希望如此，”我告诉她。

sentences.en: “I don’t know why they wouldn’t.”
sentences.zh: “我不知道他们为什么不这样做。”

blocks.html_en: As I stepped out of the exam room, I became aware of my veins pulsing and a white noise filling my head. Chest paralyzed, I didn’t scream. Instead, I paced down the hall and out the metal door to sit on the curb, waiting for breath to come.

sentences.en: As I stepped out of the exam room, I became aware of my veins pulsing and a white noise filling my head.
sentences.zh: 当我走出检查室时，我意识到我的血管在搏动，并且一种白噪声充满了我的大脑。

sentences.en: Chest paralyzed, I didn’t scream.
sentences.zh: 胸口麻木了，我没有尖叫。

sentences.en: Instead, I paced down the hall and out the metal door to sit on the curb, waiting for breath to come.
sentences.zh: 相反，我沿着大厅踱步，走出金属门，坐在路边，等待呼吸。

blocks.html_en: I had fallen in love with Randy because his optimism was magnetic. When his startup began achieving significant milestones after years of sweat equity, he had even more reason to be optimistic. I still have the photo of Randy and his co-founder posing with their first large check, their faces painted with true bliss.

sentences.en: I had fallen in love with Randy because his optimism was magnetic.
sentences.zh: 我爱上了兰迪，因为他的乐观主义很有魅力。

sentences.en: When his startup began achieving significant milestones after years of sweat equity, he had even more reason to be optimistic.
sentences.zh: 当他的初创公司经过多年的汗水投入开始实现重要的里程碑时，他更有理由保持乐观。

sentences.en: I still have the photo of Randy and his co-founder posing with their first large check, their faces painted with true bliss.
sentences.zh: 我还保存着兰迪和他的联合创始人拿着他们的第一张大支票摆姿势的照片，他们的脸上写满了真正的幸福。

blocks.html_en: Months later the success fizzled, but Randy never told me how bad things were until the demise of the business was inevitable. The way he tried to protect me from the truth created an ever-expanding distance between us. Each day he withdrew deeper inside an impenetrable shell, and my attempts to connect were met with anger.

sentences.en: Months later the success fizzled, but Randy never told me how bad things were until the demise of the business was inevitable.
sentences.zh: 几个月后，成功以失败告终，但兰迪从未告诉我事情有多糟糕，直到业务的消亡不可避免。

sentences.en: The way he tried to protect me from the truth created an ever-expanding distance between us.
sentences.zh: 他试图保护我免受真相的影响，导致我们之间的距离不断扩大。

sentences.en: Each day he withdrew deeper inside an impenetrable shell, and my attempts to connect were met with anger.
sentences.zh: 每一天，他都退缩到一个无法穿透的外壳里，而我试图与他联系的尝试却遭到了愤怒。

blocks.html_en: As a doctor I should have seen this behavior as a sign of burgeoning depression, but instead I felt wounded by his abrasive tone. I grew fearful of asking questions because they were met with irritability.

sentences.en: As a doctor I should have seen this behavior as a sign of burgeoning depression, but instead I felt wounded by his abrasive tone.
sentences.zh: 作为一名医生，我应该将这种行为视为抑郁症日益严重的征兆，但相反，我因他粗鲁的语气而感到受伤。

sentences.en: I grew fearful of asking questions because they were met with irritability.
sentences.zh: 我开始害怕提问，因为他们会感到烦躁。

blocks.html_en: One Friday morning, less than four months before the coroner’s phone call, Randy texted me from our home office: I need help. When I opened the glass French door, I was shocked to find Randy on the daybed in a fetal position.

sentences.en: One Friday morning, less than four months before the coroner’s phone call, Randy texted me from our home office: I need help.
sentences.zh: 一个周五早上，距离验尸官打电话还不到四个月，兰迪从我们的家庭办公室给我发短信：我需要帮助。

sentences.en: When I opened the glass French door, I was shocked to find Randy on the daybed in a fetal position.
sentences.zh: 当我打开法式玻璃门时，我惊讶地发现兰迪以胎儿的姿势躺在沙发床上。

blocks.html_en: “You have to hide me,” he stuttered. “The investors … they’re coming for me. You have to get me out of here.”

sentences.en: “You have to hide me,” he stuttered.
sentences.zh: “你必须把我藏起来，”他结结巴巴地说。

sentences.en: “The investors …
sentences.zh: “投资者……

sentences.en: they’re coming for me.
sentences.zh: 他们来找我了。

sentences.en: You have to get me out of here.”
sentences.zh: 你必须带我离开这里。”

blocks.html_en: “Randy, that makes no sense. Just talk to them. What’s happened?”

sentences.en: “Randy, that makes no sense.
sentences.zh: “兰迪，这毫无意义。

sentences.en: Just talk to them.
sentences.zh: 只要和他们谈谈。

sentences.en: What’s happened?”
sentences.zh: 发生了什么事？”

blocks.html_en: “I can’t do this anymore. Please kill me. Just put this pillow over my face and suffocate me, please. If you don’t do it, I will!” he threatened, grasping and shaking the pillow with both hands. His knuckles were blanching from the pressure of his grip.

sentences.en: “I can’t do this anymore.
sentences.zh: “我不能再这样做了。

sentences.en: Please kill me.
sentences.zh: 请杀了我。

sentences.en: Just put this pillow over my face and suffocate me, please.
sentences.zh: 请把这个枕头放在我脸上，让我窒息吧。

sentences.en: If you don’t do it, I will!”
sentences.zh: 你不做，我就做！”

sentences.en: he threatened, grasping and shaking the pillow with both hands.
sentences.zh: 他威胁道，双手抓住枕头并摇晃。

sentences.en: His knuckles were blanching from the pressure of his grip.
sentences.zh: 他的指关节因他的握力而发白。

blocks.html_en: Randy needed immediate psychiatric care, and as an emergency physician I knew the options. We put our trust in the mental health team at a local crisis receiving unit, and they kept Randy overnight in temporary safe housing where he could be observed. By morning they had devised a plan to transfer Randy to a retreat-like inpatient psychiatric facility that was in-network with our insurance. The psychiatrist felt Randy was very high risk, but that he could heal with eight weeks of inpatient treatment. We were told our insurance would cover it.

sentences.en: Randy needed immediate psychiatric care, and as an emergency physician I knew the options.
sentences.zh: 兰迪需要立即接受精神科护理，作为一名急诊医生，我知道有哪些选择。

sentences.en: We put our trust in the mental health team at a local crisis receiving unit, and they kept Randy overnight in temporary safe housing where he could be observed.
sentences.zh: 我们信任当地危机接收单位的心理健康团队，他们让兰迪在临时安全住所过夜，以便观察他的情况。

sentences.en: By morning they had devised a plan to transfer Randy to a retreat-like inpatient psychiatric facility that was in-network with our insurance.
sentences.zh: 到了早上，他们制定了一项计划，将兰迪转移到一个类似疗养院的精神病住院机构，该机构在我们的保险网络内。

sentences.en: The psychiatrist felt Randy was very high risk, but that he could heal with eight weeks of inpatient treatment.
sentences.zh: 精神科医生认为兰迪的风险非常高，但他可以通过八周的住院治疗康复。

sentences.en: We were told our insurance would cover it.
sentences.zh: 我们被告知我们的保险可以承保。

blocks.html_en: I remember giving Randy a long hug goodbye outside the brick intake building where family members were not allowed. I remember how we lingered, and how he clung to me. I didn’t know it would be our last embrace. He told me he felt good about this place. It seemed peaceful. He would work hard to heal, for me and for the kids.

sentences.en: I remember giving Randy a long hug goodbye outside the brick intake building where family members were not allowed.
sentences.zh: 我记得在不允许家庭成员进入的砖砌入院大楼外，我给了兰迪一个长长的拥抱告别。

sentences.en: I remember how we lingered, and how he clung to me.
sentences.zh: 我记得我们是如何徘徊的，以及他是如何紧紧抓住我的。

sentences.en: I didn’t know it would be our last embrace.
sentences.zh: 我不知道这将是我们最后的拥抱。

sentences.en: He told me he felt good about this place.
sentences.zh: 他告诉我他对这个地方感觉很好。

sentences.en: It seemed peaceful.
sentences.zh: 看起来很平静。

sentences.en: He would work hard to heal, for me and for the kids.
sentences.zh: 他会努力治愈，为了我和孩子们。

blocks.html_en: I felt good about the program, too. I had researched it heavily and agreed it was the best option.

sentences.en: I felt good about the program, too.
sentences.zh: 我对这个计划也感觉很好。

sentences.en: I had researched it heavily and agreed it was the best option.
sentences.zh: 我进行了大量研究并同意这是最好的选择。

blocks.html_en: But six days into Randy’s eight-week hospital stay, our health insurance company denied any further inpatient care. Randy had just started to feel hopeful, and he was devastated by the news. I was incredulous. How could they go against the psychiatrist’s recommendations? Couldn’t they see how risky that was?

sentences.en: But six days into Randy’s eight-week hospital stay, our health insurance company denied any further inpatient care.
sentences.zh: 但兰迪住院八周后第六天，我们的健康保险公司拒绝提供任何进一步的住院治疗。

sentences.en: Randy had just started to feel hopeful, and he was devastated by the news.
sentences.zh: 兰迪刚刚开始感到希望，但这个消息让他感到震惊。

sentences.en: I was incredulous.
sentences.zh: 我简直不敢相信。

sentences.en: How could they go against the psychiatrist’s recommendations?
sentences.zh: 他们怎么能违背精神科医生的建议呢？

sentences.en: Couldn’t they see how risky that was?
sentences.zh: 他们难道看不出这有多危险吗？

blocks.html_en: The facility appealed Randy’s case, providing psychiatrist’s notes that clearly outlined his need, but the outcome did not change. Because Randy had no prior history of mental illness, the insurance company determined he shouldn’t need to be hospitalized and stopped paying for his care. A day later Randy was forcibly discharged.

sentences.en: The facility appealed Randy’s case, providing psychiatrist’s notes that clearly outlined his need, but the outcome did not change.
sentences.zh: 该机构对兰迪的案件提出上诉，提供了精神科医生的笔记，清楚地概述了他的需求，但结果并没有改变。

sentences.en: Because Randy had no prior history of mental illness, the insurance company determined he shouldn’t need to be hospitalized and stopped paying for his care.
sentences.zh: 由于兰迪之前没有精神疾病史，保险公司认为他不需要住院，并停止支付他的护理费用。

sentences.en: A day later Randy was forcibly discharged.
sentences.zh: 一天后，兰迪被强制出院。

blocks.html_en: The ensuing months led to a rotating cycle of failed outpatient treatment programs followed by ER visits, brief admissions, and premature discharges. And Randy kept getting worse. He became so unstable with paranoid delusions and psychosis that he was not safe to be around me or the children. He never came home.

sentences.en: The ensuing months led to a rotating cycle of failed outpatient treatment programs followed by ER visits, brief admissions, and premature discharges.
sentences.zh: 接下来的几个月导致门诊治疗计划失败，然后是急诊室就诊、短暂入院和过早出院。

sentences.en: And Randy kept getting worse.
sentences.zh: 兰迪的情况越来越糟。

sentences.en: He became so unstable with paranoid delusions and psychosis that he was not safe to be around me or the children.
sentences.zh: 他因偏执妄想和精神病而变得如此不稳定，以至于他和我或孩子们在一起并不安全。

sentences.en: He never came home.
sentences.zh: 他再也没有回家。

blocks.html_en: A few days before Randy was found dead, he was supposed to have a video chat with our son. Sitting on my lap in his dinosaur pajamas, little green legs swinging back and forth as I wrapped my arms around him, our son turned to look up at me and asked, “Is Daddy too tired to talk?” I gave him a squeeze and rubbed his back. As the minutes ticked by, Randy didn’t appear on the screen. My son became heavier in my lap, his eyes drifting closed.

sentences.en: A few days before Randy was found dead, he was supposed to have a video chat with our son.
sentences.zh: 兰迪被发现死亡的前几天，他本应该和我们的儿子进行视频聊天。

sentences.en: Sitting on my lap in his dinosaur pajamas, little green legs swinging back and forth as I wrapped my arms around him, our son turned to look up at me and asked, “Is Daddy too tired to talk?”
sentences.zh: 儿子穿着恐龙睡衣坐在我的腿上，绿色的小腿来回摆动，我用双臂搂住他，儿子转身抬头看着我，问道：“爸爸是不是太累了，不想说话了？”

sentences.en: I gave him a squeeze and rubbed his back.
sentences.zh: 我捏了捏他，揉了揉他的背。

sentences.en: As the minutes ticked by, Randy didn’t appear on the screen.
sentences.zh: 随着时间的流逝，兰迪没有出现在屏幕上。

sentences.en: My son became heavier in my lap, his eyes drifting closed.
sentences.zh: 我的儿子在我腿上变得更重了，他的眼睛渐渐闭上了。

blocks.html_en: When I listen to lungs with my stethoscope, I still feel wonder at the rise and fall of each person’s breath, and my responsibility to secure safe passage to the other side of every crisis weighs heavily. But I can only control what happens inside the exam room.

sentences.en: When I listen to lungs with my stethoscope, I still feel wonder at the rise and fall of each person’s breath, and my responsibility to secure safe passage to the other side of every crisis weighs heavily.
sentences.zh: 当我用听诊器听肺部的声音时，我仍然对每个人呼吸的起伏感到惊讶，确保安全通行到每一次危机的另一边的责任重大。

sentences.en: But I can only control what happens inside the exam room.
sentences.zh: 但我只能控制考场内发生的事情。

blocks.html_en: My love for Randy and my professional experience were not enough to save him. I am a physician, and yet my husband died, proving he was sick enough to deserve care.

sentences.en: My love for Randy and my professional experience were not enough to save him.
sentences.zh: 我对兰迪的爱和我的职业经验不足以拯救他。

sentences.en: I am a physician, and yet my husband died, proving he was sick enough to deserve care.
sentences.zh: 我是一名医生，但我的丈夫去世了，证明他病得很重，值得照顾。

blocks.html_en: Now every time a patient asks me, “will this be covered by my insurance?” the feeling of panic returns.

sentences.en: Now every time a patient asks me, “will this be covered by my insurance?”
sentences.zh: 现在每次有病人问我，“我的保险可以承保吗？”

sentences.en: the feeling of panic returns.
sentences.zh: 恐慌的感觉又回来了。

blocks.html_en: With Randy in my heart, I keep advocating for what is right. During the day on insurance appeal calls. Late at night, fighting sleep to finish charts and check the right boxes so claims won’t get denied.

sentences.en: With Randy in my heart, I keep advocating for what is right.
sentences.zh: 心中有兰迪，我就一直倡导正义。

sentences.en: During the day on insurance appeal calls.
sentences.zh: 白天接到保险上诉电话。

sentences.en: Late at night, fighting sleep to finish charts and check the right boxes so claims won’t get denied.
sentences.zh: 深夜，与睡眠作斗争，完成图表并勾选正确的方框，这样索赔就不会被拒绝。

blocks.html_en: Joy Evers (née Hardison), M.D., M.P.H., is a board-certified emergency physician, Mayo Medical School graduate, host of the podcast “Your Doctor Wants to Quit,” and founder of Healthtopia Clinics, an integrative primary care practice serving over 35,000 people in Southern California.

sentences.en: Joy Evers (née Hardison), M.D., M.P.H., is a board-certified emergency physician, Mayo Medical School graduate, host of the podcast “Your Doctor Wants to Quit,” and founder of Healthtopia Clinics, an integrative primary care practice serving over 35,000 people in Southern California.
sentences.zh: Joy Evers（娘家姓 Hardison），医学博士、公共卫生硕士，是一名获得委员会认证的急诊科医生、梅奥医学院毕业生、播客“你的医生想要戒烟”的主持人，也是 Healthtopia Clinics 的创始人，这是一家综合初级保健诊所，为南加州超过 35,000 人提供服务。

blocks.html_en: If you or someone you know may be considering suicide, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline: Call or text 988 or chat 988lifeline.org. For TTY users: Use your preferred relay service or dial 711 then 988.

sentences.en: If you or someone you know may be considering suicide, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline: Call or text 988 or chat 988lifeline.org.
sentences.zh: 如果您或您认识的人可能正在考虑自杀，请联系 988 自杀和危机生命热线：致电或发短信 988 或聊天 988lifeline.org。

sentences.en: For TTY users: Use your preferred relay service or dial 711 then 988.
sentences.zh: 对于 TTY 用户：使用您首选的转接服务或先拨打 711，然后拨打 988。

content_html_zh:
我记得当验尸官的电话打来时，我站在检查室外，胸前口袋里的手机震动着。 我不想相信邻居发来的关于从我丈夫的公寓里搬走尸袋的疯狂短信，但我的来电显示上的法医办公室是明确无误的。 我接了电话，声音从我嘴里传到电话里：“你好，我是哈迪森医生。”

我是一名住在美国的急诊医生，我们拥有顶级的商业健康保险计划之一。 当我的丈夫兰迪一生中第一次出现自杀倾向时，我以为我可以为他找到最好的护理，我们的保险会支付费用，他会好起来的。 这三件事中只发生了一件。

验尸官确认是窒息自杀。 他问我是否可以发送一张最近的照片来帮助识别尸体。 “几分钟后我就可以——我有一个病人在等我。” 我把兰迪肿胀的脸的侵入性视觉放入我脑海中的一个隔间中，与手头的任务隔离开来。

检查室门的另一边是一个蹒跚学步的孩子，靠在母亲的怀里，用孔雀鱼般的呼吸声咳嗽着。 我的表情重新变得平静，我向母亲和孩子打招呼，同时我的眼睛飞快地盯着显示器，显示低氧和快速脉搏——呼吸窘迫的迹象。 我给孩子吸氧，并要求进行呼吸治疗和胸部 X 光检查。 当我听她的肺部声音时，小女孩握住我的食指，我们惊讶地看着对方。 她胸口的每一次起伏都像是一个奇迹。

我在那个考场里感觉比在外面的世界更安全。 我知道在那个房间里要做什么，在那个房间里，我可以让事情变得更好。 在那个房间之外，所有的赌注都被取消了。

“我想对她进行流感和呼吸道合胞病毒检测，”我对母亲说。

“我们的保险可以承保吗？” 她用嘴对我说，以免她的女儿听到。

“我希望如此，”我告诉她。 “我不知道他们为什么不这样做。”

当我走出检查室时，我意识到我的血管在搏动，并且一种白噪声充满了我的大脑。 胸口麻木了，我没有尖叫。 相反，我沿着大厅踱步，走出金属门，坐在路边，等待呼吸。

我爱上了兰迪，因为他的乐观主义很有魅力。 当他的初创公司经过多年的汗水投入开始实现重要的里程碑时，他更有理由保持乐观。 我还保存着兰迪和他的联合创始人拿着他们的第一张大支票摆姿势的照片，他们的脸上写满了真正的幸福。

几个月后，成功以失败告终，但兰迪从未告诉我事情有多糟糕，直到业务的消亡不可避免。 他试图保护我免受真相的影响，导致我们之间的距离不断扩大。 每一天，他都退缩到一个无法穿透的外壳里，而我试图与他联系的尝试却遭到了愤怒。

作为一名医生，我应该将这种行为视为抑郁症日益严重的征兆，但相反，我因他粗鲁的语气而感到受伤。 我开始害怕提问，因为他们会感到烦躁。

一个周五早上，距离验尸官打电话还不到四个月，兰迪从我们的家庭办公室给我发短信：我需要帮助。 当我打开法式玻璃门时，我惊讶地发现兰迪以胎儿的姿势躺在沙发床上。

“你必须把我藏起来，”他结结巴巴地说。 “投资者…… 他们来找我了。 你必须带我离开这里。”

“兰迪，这毫无意义。 只要和他们谈谈。 发生了什么事？”

“我不能再这样做了。 请杀了我。 请把这个枕头放在我脸上，让我窒息吧。 你不做，我就做！” 他威胁道，双手抓住枕头并摇晃。 他的指关节因他的握力而发白。

兰迪需要立即接受精神科护理，作为一名急诊医生，我知道有哪些选择。 我们信任当地危机接收单位的心理健康团队，他们让兰迪在临时安全住所过夜，以便观察他的情况。 到了早上，他们制定了一项计划，将兰迪转移到一个类似疗养院的精神病住院机构，该机构在我们的保险网络内。 精神科医生认为兰迪的风险非常高，但他可以通过八周的住院治疗康复。 我们被告知我们的保险可以承保。

我记得在不允许家庭成员进入的砖砌入院大楼外，我给了兰迪一个长长的拥抱告别。 我记得我们是如何徘徊的，以及他是如何紧紧抓住我的。 我不知道这将是我们最后的拥抱。 他告诉我他对这个地方感觉很好。 看起来很平静。 他会努力治愈，为了我和孩子们。

我对这个计划也感觉很好。 我进行了大量研究并同意这是最好的选择。

但兰迪住院八周后第六天，我们的健康保险公司拒绝提供任何进一步的住院治疗。 兰迪刚刚开始感到希望，但这个消息让他感到震惊。 我简直不敢相信。 他们怎么能违背精神科医生的建议呢？ 他们难道看不出这有多危险吗？

该机构对兰迪的案件提出上诉，提供了精神科医生的笔记，清楚地概述了他的需求，但结果并没有改变。 由于兰迪之前没有精神疾病史，保险公司认为他不需要住院，并停止支付他的护理费用。 一天后，兰迪被强制出院。

接下来的几个月导致门诊治疗计划失败，然后是急诊室就诊、短暂入院和过早出院。 兰迪的情况越来越糟。 他因偏执妄想和精神病而变得如此不稳定，以至于他和我或孩子们在一起并不安全。 他再也没有回家。

兰迪被发现死亡的前几天，他本应该和我们的儿子进行视频聊天。 儿子穿着恐龙睡衣坐在我的腿上，绿色的小腿来回摆动，我用双臂搂住他，儿子转身抬头看着我，问道：“爸爸是不是太累了，不想说话了？” 我捏了捏他，揉了揉他的背。 随着时间的流逝，兰迪没有出现在屏幕上。 我的儿子在我腿上变得更重了，他的眼睛渐渐闭上了。

当我用听诊器听肺部的声音时，我仍然对每个人呼吸的起伏感到惊讶，确保安全通行到每一次危机的另一边的责任重大。 但我只能控制考场内发生的事情。

我对兰迪的爱和我的职业经验不足以拯救他。 我是一名医生，但我的丈夫去世了，证明他病得很重，值得照顾。

现在每次有病人问我，“我的保险可以承保吗？” 恐慌的感觉又回来了。

心中有兰迪，我就一直倡导正义。 白天接到保险上诉电话。 深夜，与睡眠作斗争，完成图表并勾选正确的方框，这样索赔就不会被拒绝。

Joy Evers（娘家姓 Hardison），医学博士、公共卫生硕士，是一名获得委员会认证的急诊科医生、梅奥医学院毕业生、播客“你的医生想要戒烟”的主持人，也是 Healthtopia Clinics 的创始人，这是一家综合初级保健诊所，为南加州超过 35,000 人提供服务。

如果您或您认识的人可能正在考虑自杀，请联系 988 自杀和危机生命热线：致电或发短信 988 或聊天 988lifeline.org。 对于 TTY 用户：使用您首选的转接服务或先拨打 711，然后拨打 988。
